name: Joanne Liszewski
bio: I've been building since 2014, starting with GUI interfaces in C++ and JavaScript, and because technology evolves so rapidly, I never stop learning. That early focus on user-centered design grew into full-stack development: React, Next.js, AI workflows, and accessible experiences shaped by my lived experience as a visually impaired developer. What drives me is seeing something I built make a difference. I specialize in apps that uplift communities, like Be In The Know: plain-English legal rights education for reentry, powered by JO, an AI assistant citing only human-reviewed legal sources.
proudestAccomplishment: Turning a self-authored audit into action almost overnight. On July 11th I wrote a full gap analysis against my own README — flagging that the embedding provider would be unreachable in production, quotas were not enforced, cron routes were spoofable, and chat history vanished on refresh. Every one of those was fixed within a day, without loosening the constitution I set for JO: no answer without an approved, cited source, and no content entering the corpus without a human approving it. Watching that discipline hold — architecture-level guardrails that survive rapid iteration, backed by a test suite and CI that catch regressions — while turning a solo project into something a vulnerable population can actually trust, is what I'm proudest of.

project.name: Be in the Know
project.tagline: "Be In The Know is a free web app that turns the legal rights of 20 million Americans with records into plain English — with an AI guide, JO, that answers only from the law as it's currently on the books in your state, and cites it."
project.targetAudience: Be In The Know is built for justice-impacted people navigating reentry, anyone on parole or probation, rebuilding after incarceration, or facing barriers to voting, housing, or work,  plus the families and support organizations standing with them.
project.problem: People navigating reentry face fifty states' worth of rules about voting, expungement, housing, employment, and supervision. The information is public — but scattered, written at a college reading level, and different in every state. Most people act on rumor or assume the answer is no, and quietly give up rights the law already gives them. Generic AI chatbots make it worse: they hallucinate statutes with confidence, and here a wrong answer can cost someone their vote, home, or job. Be In The Know turns each state's current law into plain, sixth-grade-level English through JO, an assistant built so it can't guess: it answers only from a human-reviewed corpus of official law, cites every source, and refers users to legal aid when it doesn't know.
keyFeatures: Source-grounded chat that can't guess: JO answers exclusively from retrieved, human-approved statute text and plain-English summaries — never from model memory. Every answer cites its sources, an output guard replaces any uncited substantive answer with a response built directly from the sources, and JO refuses and routes to legal aid when no approved source covers the question.

Human-gated content pipeline with legislative monitoring: an automated weekly pipeline watches legislative activity in all 50 states (LegiScan) and flags laws that may have changed into a review queue — but nothing enters JO's answer corpus until a person reviews and approves it. Automation fetches, chunks, and embeds; only a human can approve.

Privacy-first design for a vulnerable population: anonymous by default with server-enforced daily quotas, no raw IP addresses stored (salted hashes only), no tracking cookies — plus optional free accounts with private cross-device chat history and one-click permanent deletion of everything.
project.biggestChallenge: Making an LLM trustworthy enough for legal information. Telling the model to be careful isn't enough — correctness had to be architectural: retrieval-first design where the model never decides what the law is, model-labeled vector search so mixed embeddings can't corrupt retrieval, an output guard that rejects ungrounded answers, prompt-injection hardening proven by a red-team battery, and a review gate automation can't bypass. The hard part wasn't making JO answer — it was making it refuse.
project.futurePlans: Spanish-language JO, on a language-agnostic foundation. Our embedding model already maps Spanish questions into the same vector space as the English legal corpus, so retrieval needs no redesign — the build is generation: JO answers in the user's language while still drawing only from approved English law and citing every source, with a disclaimer on every translated answer that the official source is in English, plus a persistent language toggle and fully translated UI.

name: Jacqueline Delgado
bio: Three decades in operations and human services taught me the expensive failures are the quiet ones. Now I build with AI, which mostly means learning whose conventions I inherited: calm returns violet because someone decided calm was violet, and "reliable data" is faithful to a moment that's already passed. Away from the keyboard I keep bees, and at it I build for the problems I can reach. I scrutinize the input harder than the output, not because I distrust the model but because I know what it's reflecting back.
proudestAccomplishment: The part I am most proud of is the architectural design — the two tables, the communication between the two apps, the theme, the color choice, the layout, and the features. Also running Vitest on the logic, because it showed me the poor behavior my vibe-coded version 2 had.
lessonLearned: I learned that planning matters. It saves me from debugging a lot, and headaches later. Creating md files not only to reference, but also to guide and correct understanding is imperative. It was incredibly easy to create the UI with the md files; it had the color choice, the pin choice, the font choice, the position, the layout. The mark down files save so much time. Although, md files takes time to read in VS code and edit, and they get long, mark down files help organize my thoughts.

project.name: Narley
project.tagline: Every pin on Narley's app was entered by the provider that's standing behind it. Enter your zip code and find shelter, food, employment opportunity or resources that are actually there.
project.targetAudience: The apps are for justice-impacted people seeking shelter, food, employment, and resources after release. And providers and reentry organizations — where one edit replaces 20 phone calls and a wasted trip. Notes are as detailed as the provider likes.
project.problem:
Resource information moves slower than the resources do. A job fair, an apartment opening, a landlord taking applications — those windows are measured in days, and word goes out by mail or by a provider making 20 to 50 calls. It arrives after it's over.
The second problem is staleness. A mobile clinic changes hours. A tent drive runs out on the 20th but restarts in August. A car repair fund closes in June, reopens August 1st. Nobody updates the flyer, so people ride across town to a closed door.
The third is that the relationship ends at the appointment. Narley keeps it open: the provider edits the pin and leaves a note, the reader sees what's true today, saves the resource, and sets a reminder for the date it comes back. Goodwill of Southern New England staff shaped half the categories.
keyFeatures: Providers edit their own pins in real time. When a resource fills, changes hours, or closes for the season, one edit updates everyone holding the app — replacing 20 to 50 phone calls and the trips those calls were meant to prevent.

A +Custom category for resources no taxonomy predicts — a tent, a coat drive, a one-day repair fund. Providers attach notes as detailed as they want: out until August 1st, accepting applications again in the fall, hours changed this week.

Readers search by ZIP, save a resource, and set reminders for the date it reopens. A fund closed in June with a note saying August 1st becomes a reminder on August 1st — so a dead window turns into a return date instead of a wasted trip.
project.biggestChallenge: The hardest part wasn't the data — it was the AI. When a reader reported a resource closed, the model went hunting for evidence and found the reliable-but-stale sources: the live directory listing, the cached hours, the site that still resolves. Then it argued the reader was wrong. It wanted the org open because the internet still says so. I had to script the trust hierarchy explicitly: the person standing at the door outranks the website. Reliable isn't current, and the model had to be told.
project.futurePlans: Two things, both blocked by paid Google APIs rather than by design. Translation at read time, not write time: a case manager pins in English, the reader sees the language they've set. The provider doesn't have to be bilingual to serve a bilingual client. And in-app directions — walking, biking, transit, driving on the Expo map, instead of sending people out to Google Maps. For someone without a car, the bus route is the difference between a resource that exists and one they can reach.

name: Destiny Mills
bio: Hi, I’m Destiny Mills, a full-stack software developer with a background in legal support and paralegal studies. I’m passionate about building AI-powered tools that solve real-world problems, especially in legal access, education, and healthcare. I enjoy turning complex workflows into intuitive, user-centered applications that make information more accessible and meaningful
proudestAccomplishment: I’m most proud of building an AI-powered legal education platform around a real problem I experienced firsthand. The application uses a structured intake process to generate personalized legal research roadmaps, translates complex legal language into plain English, and supports both individual and correctional institution users through secure role-based access and AI integration.
lessonLearned: I learned that solving a real user problem is more important than adding features. AI is a powerful tool, but it works best when paired with thoughtful system design, user feedback, and clear guardrails to ensure the information is educational, accurate, and easy to understand.

project.name: CaseCompass AI
project.tagline: CaseCompass AI is an AI-powered legal education platform that personalizes legal research, translates complex case law into plain language, and helps users confidently navigate the legal system.
project.targetAudience: CaseCompass AI is designed for individuals navigating the legal system, correctional institutions, legal aid organizations, educators, and anyone who needs legal information explained in clear, understandable language.
project.problem: CaseCompass AI addresses the gap between access to legal information and the ability to understand it. Millions of self-represented individuals—including incarcerated people, legal aid clients, and those who cannot afford an attorney—struggle to navigate complex legal research, court opinions, and legal terminology. Existing research tools assume legal knowledge, leaving many without meaningful guidance. CaseCompass AI transforms legal research into an educational, step-by-step experience that helps users understand the law, organize their research, and confidently navigate the legal system.
keyFeatures: AI-guided legal intake that builds a personalized legal research roadmap.
Plain-language explanations of court opinions, legal terminology, and case citations.
Educational legal research tools with source citations that teach users how to research—not replace an attorney.
project.biggestChallenge:
Building an AI platform that simplifies complex legal information while remaining accurate, educational, and transparent. The system must avoid giving legal advice, ground every response in authoritative legal sources, adapt explanations to different literacy levels, and maintain security for both public and correctional institution deployments. Balancing usability with legal reliability has been the project’s biggest challenge.

These responses highlight the educational mission, technical complexity, and real-world impact qualities that tend to resonate well with both technical reviewers and potential industry partners.
project.futurePlans: Given another month, I would integrate Westlaw alongside CaseCompass AI so users could seamlessly move between AI-guided research and primary legal sources. I would also add bilingual support to make the platform accessible to a broader population, helping overcome both language and literacy barriers while improving meaningful access to legal research.

name: Summer Halsey
bio: I'm a full-stack engineer passionate about building software that solves meaningful real-world problems. My approach starts with architecture, documentation, and thoughtful system design before implementation, using AI to improve engineering quality rather than replace engineering judgment. I'm especially interested in developing secure, maintainable applications that make complex information easier for people to understand and use.
proudestAccomplishment: I'm most proud of building an AI system designed for trust. By grounding every response in users uploaded documents and prioritizing privacy from the start, Verity is better suited for high-consequence situations than a generic AI chatbot.
lessonLearned: I learned that good architecture gives AI direction. The more clearly I defined the system, constraints, and requirements upfront, the better the AI-assisted development process became.

project.name: Verity
project.tagline: Don't lose your freedom to a technicality. Upload your documents, ask anything, and stop the guesswork.
project.targetAudience: Individuals on probation or parole, especially those managing multiple supervision documents across counties or states. Verity also supports attorneys, case managers, reentry organizations, and family members assisting with successful reentry.
project.problem: Probation and parole documents are often written in dense legal language that's difficult to understand during an already stressful transition back into the community. Missing a reporting requirement, misunderstanding a travel restriction, or overlooking a deadline can have serious consequences. While AI has made legal information more accessible, generic chatbots can hallucinate or provide inaccurate guidance—creating unnecessary risk in high-consequence situations. Verity addresses this by helping users understand their own supervision documents through plain-language, source-grounded explanations tied directly to the documents they've uploaded.
keyFeatures: 1. OCR-Powered Document Processing  Upload supervision documents from your phone or computer. Verity automatically extracts, organizes, and structures the content, making complex legal paperwork searchable and easier to navigate.

2. Source-Grounded AI Chat

Ask questions about one or multiple uploaded documents at the same time. Every response is grounded in the user's documents—not the AI's memory—to provide explanations tied directly to the original source.

3. Privacy-First Document Workspace  Manage supervision documents in one secure workspace with ownership-based access, optional accounts, and automatic cleanup for anonymous sessions to help protect sensitive personal information.
project.biggestChallenge: The biggest challenge was designing an AI system people could trust in high-consequence situations. Rather than relying on a generic chatbot, I built Verity around source-grounded responses tied directly to uploaded documents, while treating privacy, document ownership, OCR quality, and secure data handling as architectural requirements instead of optional features.
project.futurePlans: I'd expand Verity beyond probation and parole by supporting additional legally binding documents such as custody agreements, rental contracts, employment agreements, and court orders. I'd also improve OCR accuracy, multilingual support, and document organization so users can securely manage multiple documents in one workspace and ask questions across them with greater confidence.
